area: Audio transport core (paced playout, outbound pipeline, playout progress, resample, framing, browser/telephony egress, websocket lifecycle)
how_it_works: TTS provider adapters push one `tts.audio` packet per provider websocket frame with no size cap (packages/voice-tts-cartesia/src/index.ts:248-260, packages/voice-tts-deepgram/src/index.ts:227-251). `wireTelephonyOutboundPipeline` subscribes to `tts.audio` and, per packet, calls `callbacks.encodeFrames(...)` then `playout.enqueue(frames)` in a single call (packages/voice-server-websocket/src/outbound-playout-pipeline.ts:98-118). `encodeFrames` slices the whole packet into 20ms frames up front and returns the entire array (packages/voice-server-websocket/src/index.ts:475-540; twilio.ts:199-210). `PacedPlayoutQueue.enqueue` checks `queuedDurationMs + additionalDurationMs > maxQueuedOutputAudioMs` and, on overflow, `clear()`s the whole queue and fires `onOverflow` (packages/voice-server-websocket/src/paced-playout.ts:40-52); the browser/telephony `onOverflow` calls `onStop('overflow')` and closes the socket with 1013 (outbound-playout-pipeline.ts:59-63). The pump uses drift-corrected `setTimeout` against an absolute `nextDeadlineMs` deadline, re-baselining to 0 on natural drain and on clear (paced-playout.ts:114-150). `PlayoutProgressEmitter` accumulates per-context played-out ms off `onFramePlayed` and emits throttled `tts.playout_progress` (playout-progress.ts:27-44). The default interactive queue was lowered to 200ms across all four transports (index.ts:144, twilio.ts:109, smartpbx.ts:102) per VE-01.3, while frames remain 20ms.

findings.title: 200ms output queue + whole-packet enqueue: a single normal-size TTS chunk overflows and kills the call
findings.evidence: DEFAULT_MAX_QUEUED_OUTPUT_AUDIO_MS = 200 (index.ts:144) with DEFAULT_OUTBOUND_FRAME_DURATION_MS = 20 means the queue holds at most 10 frames. But each `tts.audio` packet is enqueued atomically: in outbound-playout-pipeline.ts:112-118 `const frames = callbacks.encodeFrames(...); playout.enqueue(frames);`, and encodeFrames returns ALL 20ms frames for the whole packet (index.ts:535-539: `for (let offset = 0; offset < resampled.byteLength; offset += frameBytesCount) { ... pushWireFrame(...) }`). enqueue rejects the whole batch when it exceeds the cap: `if (this.queuedDurationMs + additionalDurationMs > this.maxQueuedOutputAudioMs) { const discardedDurationMs = this.clear(); this.onOverflow(discardedDurationMs); return false; }` (paced-playout.ts:43-46). The browser/telephony onOverflow then tears down the connection: `(discardedMs) => { callbacks.onStop('overflow'); recordDiscardedPlayout(discardedMs, 'overflow'); closeWebSocketWithFallback(socket, 1013, 'outbound audio queue exceeded'); }` (outbound-playout-pipeline.ts:59-63). Providers routinely emit >200ms of audio in one frame — Cartesia/Deepgram push each provider websocket frame unchunked (cartesia index.ts:260, deepgram index.ts:250) — so the FIRST audio packet of an ordinary sentence (e.g. 1.5s) produces ~75 frames = ~1500ms additionalDurationMs > 200ms, overflowing immediately, discarding the whole turn, and closing the socket. The 200ms cap was added without re-chunking provider packets into <=200ms enqueue batches.
findings.recommendation: Decouple the back-pressure cap from the enqueue granularity. Either (a) re-chunk in the pipeline: feed encodeFrames output to the queue in <=maxQueuedOutputAudioMs slices and let the pacer drain between slices (the pacer already drains in realtime, so this is natural flow control), or (b) have enqueue accept oversized bursts by trimming/backpressuring rather than clear()+close — overflow should mean 'producer outran realtime playout by a sustained margin', not 'one normal chunk arrived'. A 200ms cap is only safe if no single enqueue call can exceed it; size the cap against the largest expected single provider packet, or buffer provider audio and meter it into the queue. Add a test at the production 200ms default (current outbound-playout-pipeline.test.ts:47 uses 30_000) feeding a single >200ms encodeFrames result and assert the call is NOT torn down.
findings.checklist_ref: VE-01.3 (bounded playout buffer 100-200ms unless opt-in); GAP-ANALYSIS §1 'bounded playout jitter buffer and real-time pacing'

findings.title: Overflow path at 200ms default is untested; pipeline test pins maxQueuedOutputAudioMs to 30000
findings.evidence: The only outbound-pipeline test wires the queue with `maxQueuedOutputAudioMs: 30_000` (line 47) and an encodeFrames that returns a SINGLE frame regardless of input: `encodeFrames: (audio) => [{ contextId: 'turn-drain', send: () => true }]` (lines 52-55). With a 30s cap and one frame per packet, the overflow branch can never fire, so the production-relevant interaction (200ms cap vs multi-frame provider packets, Finding 1) has zero coverage. paced-playout.test.ts likewise always uses 10_000ms caps (e.g. line 24, 197) and hand-built single frames, never the production framing pipeline. The regression that lowered the default to 200ms therefore shipped with no test exercising a realistic large chunk against it.
findings.recommendation: Add a pipeline-level test using the real encodeFrames (or one that emits N>10 20ms frames for a single tts.audio) with maxQueuedOutputAudioMs=200, asserting playout proceeds and the socket is not closed with 1013. Keep an explicit overflow test that drives a SUSTAINED producer (many packets faster than realtime) to assert overflow/close, distinguishing it from the single-large-chunk case.
findings.checklist_ref: VE-01.3 acceptance test; §12 'never claim done without proof'

findings.title: Opus downlink flushes (zero-pads) on every tts.audio packet, injecting silence mid-sentence
findings.evidence: encodeFrames sets `const flush = offset + frameBytesCount >= resampled.byteLength;` (index.ts:527) for the LAST sub-frame of every packet and passes it to `opusCodec.encodePcm16Frame(samples, flush)` (line 528). In browser-opus.ts, flush zero-pads the leftover partial frame to a full 960-sample frame and resets the carry: `if (flush && remainder.length > 0) { const padded = new Int16Array(frameSamples); padded.set(remainder); frames.push(encoder.encode(...padded...)); encodeRemainder = new Int16Array(0); }` (browser-opus.ts:44-48). Cartesia/Deepgram emit many tts.audio packets per sentence (cartesia index.ts:260, deepgram index.ts:250) that are not aligned to 20ms@48k boundaries, so every packet boundary that leaves a partial frame emits a zero-padded (silence) frame and discards the carry. The `encodeRemainder` mechanism that exists specifically to carry partial samples ACROSS packets (browser-opus.ts:32-43, 49-51) is defeated, producing audible clicks/discontinuities between intra-sentence chunks.
findings.recommendation: Only flush at true end-of-context (tts.end / drain), not per tts.audio packet. Carry the opus encoder remainder across packets within a turn and flush once when the turn's audio is complete, so partial frames concatenate instead of being silence-padded. This restores the purpose of encodeRemainder.
findings.checklist_ref: GAP-ANALYSIS §1 'fixed small outbound chunks / re-chunk TTS frames'; §5 streaming TTS continuity

findings.title: Single per-connection resampler map shared across input, output, and opus directions
findings.evidence: One `streamingResamplers: new Map()` per connection (index.ts:184) is threaded into inbound resampling (`resampleAudioBytes(binaryAudio.audio, binaryAudio.sampleRateHz, inputSampleRateHz, streamingResamplers)` index.ts:622, and the JSON-audio path index.ts:670), outbound resampling (`resampleAudioBytes(audio, sourceSampleRateHz, outputSampleRateHz, streamingResamplers)` index.ts:476), and opus pre-encode resampling (`resamplePcm16Streaming(streamingResamplers, samples, outputSampleRateHz, opusCodec.sampleRateHz)` index.ts:525). resamplePcm16Streaming keys solely by `${source}->${target}` (resample.ts:170) and the StreamingPcm16Resampler retains FIR history across calls (resample.ts:111-159). At default rates the direction keys differ (inbound 48000->16000, opus 16000->48000) so there is no collision today, but the map mixes unrelated audio streams under one key namespace with no per-direction/per-context isolation. If inputSampleRateHz and outputSampleRateHz are ever configured to the same value (or any config makes two directions share a src->tgt pair), inbound mic audio and outbound TTS audio would share one resampler's history buffer, corrupting both.
findings.recommendation: Use separate resampler maps (or key prefixes) for inbound vs outbound/opus paths so the src->tgt key space cannot collide across directions. This is currently latent, not active at default rates; fix to prevent a config change from silently cross-contaminating streams.

summary: The transport core is well-engineered in most respects: the PacedPlayoutQueue's drift-corrected absolute-deadline pacing with natural-drain re-baselining is correct and well-tested (paced-playout.ts:114-150, paced-playout.test.ts), the interruptible/uninterruptible frame taxonomy never replays speech after barge-in (clearInterruptible, paced-playout.ts:82-102 with strong tests), the FIR anti-alias downsampler and streaming-history resampler are sound (resample.ts), heartbeat/max-session/startup-timeout lifecycle and graceful drain are solid (websocket-lifecycle.ts, index.ts:364-396), and playout-progress is the authoritative clock as designed. BUT the VE-01.3 change that lowered the output queue to 200ms (index.ts:144 and the three telephony servers) is a ship-breaker as integrated: provider TTS packets are enqueued whole (one enqueue per tts.audio packet) while a single ordinary-sentence packet exceeds 200ms, so PacedPlayoutQueue.enqueue overflows on the first chunk, clears the turn, and closes the socket with 1013 (paced-playout.ts:43-46 -> outbound-playout-pipeline.ts:59-63). This is untested because the pipeline test pins the cap to 30000ms and emits one frame per packet (outbound-playout-pipeline.test.ts:47,52-55). The 200ms cap must be decoupled from enqueue granularity (re-chunk into <=cap slices, or make overflow mean sustained producer-outrun rather than one big chunk) before this can ship. Secondary: the opus downlink flush-per-packet injects mid-sentence silence (index.ts:527 / browser-opus.ts:44-48), and the per-connection resampler map is shared across directions (latent collision risk).
